old_claim.note: Craft Quality Marks provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct edge remains because the scoped water-powered oil-press node is about watermills, presses, and crop inputs; institutional craft or hallmark quality marks are a later certification practice and are not part of the node's 1000-century technology boundary.

source_shape.source_locator: The Eggares Olive Press Museum describes a Byzantine-era water-powered olive mill using water as the power source from around the tenth century, without making craft quality marks part of the press technology.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports water power and olive-milling machinery as the relevant oil-press lineage rather than institutional craft marking.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Once craft_quality_marks is dated to the 13th-century hallmarking and craft-inspection scope, it cannot be a prerequisite for a 1000-century water-powered press whose evidence is mechanical and agricultural.

ontology_before: Water-Powered Oil Presses depended directly on Craft Quality Marks, implying institutional quality marks enabled water-driven oil extraction.
ontology_after: Water-Powered Oil Presses depends on watermills and crop landrace selection; craft quality marks remain a separate institutional certification lineage.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: craft_quality_marks is no longer a direct prerequisite for water_powered_oil_presses.
would_reject_if: The water_powered_oil_presses node were narrowed to later guild-inspected or branded oil production.
A stronger source showed tenth-century water-powered oil presses required formal craft quality marks.
craft_quality_marks were broadened back to generic workshop practice rather than institutional certification marks and inspections.
short_reason: Water-powered oil presses depend on mills and crops, not institutional craft marks.
